9	# Feature: Git Awareness and Revision Control Integration
10	
11	**ID:** FEAT-012
12	**Status:** Complete
13	**Priority:** P1
14	**Owner:** DDx Team
15	
16	## Overview
17	
18	DDx operates on files that live in git repositories. Several DDx capabilities
19	require git awareness beyond "git is the user's problem":
20	
21	- Protecting spec documents from loss during rapid agent editing
22	- Tracking document changes for the staleness model
23	- Enabling MCP/UI clients to commit edits they make to documents
24	- Providing document history to agents inspecting how specs evolved
25	
26	DDx is not replacing git. It is adding a thin, deliberate integration layer
27	that makes git operations available where DDx operations need them — commit
28	checkpointing, history queries, and safe write-then-commit workflows.
29	
30	## Problem Statement
31	
32	**Current situation:**
33	- Agents edit spec documents rapidly, sometimes overwriting each other's work
34	  or losing changes when context windows reset
35	- `ddx doc stamp` records content hashes but has no awareness of when a
36	  document was last committed vs merely saved
37	- The MCP server is read-only; a client that wants to edit a document via MCP
38	  has no way to commit the change
39	- The web UI (FEAT-008) can't offer "save" functionality without a commit path
40	- Document history requires manual `git log` — no DDx surface for it
41	
42	**Desired outcome:** DDx operations that modify tracked files can auto-commit
43	to protect work. MCP and UI clients can write and commit documents through
44	DDx. Agents can query document revision history to understand how specs
45	evolved.
46	
47	## User Scenarios
48	
49	### S1: Agent Rapidly Editing Spec Documents (early commit)
50	
51	An agent is evolving multiple spec documents in a `helix evolve` session. It
52	updates the PRD, two feature specs, and a solution design. Between each edit,
53	DDx should checkpoint the change to git so that:
54	
55	- If the agent crashes or the session ends, no edits are lost
56	- Each document's change is individually attributable in history
57	- Concurrent agents on other branches don't conflict with uncommitted state
58	
59	**Trigger:** Any DDx operation that writes to a tracked document (e.g.,
60	`ddx doc stamp`, future `ddx doc edit`, MCP write tools) should offer or
61	default to auto-commit.
62	
63	**Design constraint:** Early commits should be lightweight (no hooks, fast).
64	The user or workflow can squash/rebase later. The important thing is that
65	changes survive.
66	
67	### S2: Document Graph Tracking Changes Over Time
68	
69	`ddx doc stale` currently computes staleness from content hashes recorded in
70	frontmatter stamps. But the interesting question is often "when did this
71	document last change relative to its dependencies?" — which requires git
72	history.
73	
74	**Scenarios:**
75	- "Show me documents that changed in the last 5 commits" — useful for
76	  detecting what's in play
77	- "When was this document last modified?" — git log, not file mtime
78	- "What changed between the current stamp and the current content?" — diff
79	  from stamped hash to working copy
80	- "Which documents changed since tag v0.2.0?" — release-scoped staleness
81	
82	**Design constraint:** These are read queries against git history. DDx wraps
83	them with document-graph semantics (artifact IDs, dependency relationships)
84	rather than raw file paths.
85	
86	### S3: MCP Client Edits a Document
87	
88	An agent connected via MCP wants to update a feature spec. Today the server
89	is read-only. With git awareness:
90	
91	1. Agent calls `ddx_doc_write` with artifact ID and new content
92	2. DDx writes the file to disk
93	3. DDx commits the change with a structured message (e.g., "docs: update
94	   FEAT-001 via MCP [agent: claude]")
95	4. The commit is immediately visible to other tools reading the repo
96	
97	**Design constraint:** Write-then-commit must be atomic from the client's
98	perspective. If the commit fails, the write should be rolled back (or at
99	minimum, clearly reported as uncommitted).
100	
101	### S4: Web UI Saves a Document Edit
102	
103	A developer editing a document in the FEAT-008 web UI clicks "Save". This
104	is the same flow as S3 but via HTTP instead of MCP:
105	
106	1. UI sends `PUT /api/docs/:id` with new content
107	2. Server writes the file
108	3. Server commits with attribution
109	4. UI shows "Saved and committed" or an error
110	
111	### S5: Agent Inspects Document Evolution
112	
113	An agent working on a feature wants to understand how the governing spec
114	evolved — what requirements were added, what changed, why. This is more
115	than "is it stale?" — it's "what happened?"
116	
117	**Scenarios:**
118	- "Show me the last 5 changes to FEAT-001" — git log filtered by file path
119	- "What changed in FEAT-001 between v0.1.0 and now?" — diff between tags
120	- "Who last edited this document?" — git blame
121	- "Show me all documents that changed in this branch" — branch diff
122	
123	### S6: Workflow Checkpoints Before and After Phases
124	
125	A workflow tool wants to tag a known-good state before entering a
126	risky phase (e.g., before `helix build` modifies code). DDx can provide:
127	
128	- `ddx checkpoint "pre-build"` — creates a lightweight git tag
129	- `ddx checkpoint --restore "pre-build"` — restores to that state
130	
131	This is a thin wrapper over git tags, but named in DDx terms.
132	
133	### S7: Bead Tracker Changes Are Committed
134	
135	Agents create, update, and close beads throughout a work session. The bead
136	tracker file (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`) is project state that must be committed to
137	git — it records what work exists, who claimed it, and what's done.
138	
139	**Current problem:** `.ddx/beads.jsonl` is not gitignored, but nothing in DDx
140	ensures it gets committed. Agents that don't know about this convention leave
141	bead changes uncommitted, leading to lost tracker state when sessions end or
142	branches switch.
143	
144	**Required behavior:**
145	- DDx bead-mutating commands (`ddx bead create`, `update`, `close`, etc.)
146	  should auto-commit `beads.jsonl` after successful mutations (governed by the
147	  same `git.auto_commit` setting as document changes)
148	- `ddx install` should auto-commit plugin-related changes (skill symlinks,
149	  bead tracker updates)
150	- `ddx init` should generate agent guidance (in CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md) that
151	  instructs agents to commit `beads.jsonl` alongside code changes
152	
153	**Design constraint:** Bead auto-commits should be lightweight. A single bead
154	mutation produces one commit. Batch operations (e.g., `ddx bead import`)
155	produce one commit for the whole batch.
156	
157	### S8: Agent Guidance Generated on Init
158	
159	`ddx init` should produce agent-facing guidance that ensures agents commit
160	DDx-managed state files. Without this, agents treat `.ddx/beads.jsonl` and

9	# Solution Design: Task Execution Lifecycle
10	
11	## Purpose
12	
13	This design turns FEAT-010's task execution lifecycle into an implementation
14	shape. It is intentionally below the feature spec and above code-level storage
15	types: enough detail to keep the `run`, `try`, and `work` layers aligned
16	without re-opening the routing boundary in FEAT-006.
17	
18	## Layer Model
19	
20	Task execution is composed from named layers:
21	
22	| Layer | Public verb | Meaning |
23	| --- | --- | --- |
24	| 1 | `ddx run` | one upstream agent invocation |
25	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | one bead attempt in an isolated worktree |
26	| 3 | `ddx work` | one mechanical queue-drain loop |
27	
28	Each higher layer references child records from the layer beneath it. Comparison,
29	replay, benchmark, review, and artifact-regeneration workflows are compositions
30	over the task execution lifecycle, not new run kinds.
31	
32	## Record Shape
33	
34	Every record lives under `.ddx/runs/<run-id>/record.json`. Large bodies are
35	attachments in the same directory.
36	
37	```json
38	{
39	  "run_id": "run_20260430T120102Z_01HV...",
40	  "layer": 2,
41	  "parent_run_id": "run_20260430T115900Z_01HV...",
42	  "started_at": "2026-04-30T12:01:02Z",
43	  "finished_at": "2026-04-30T12:04:12Z",
44	  "terminal_status": "success",
45	  "actor": "erik",
46	  "host": "sindri",
47	  "git_revision": "abc123",
48	  "ddx_version": "0.0.0-dev",
49	  "produces_artifact": null,
50	  "attachments": [
51	    {"name": "prompt", "path": "prompt.md", "media_type": "text/markdown"},
52	    {"name": "result", "path": "result.json", "media_type": "application/json"}
53	  ],
54	  "layer1": null,
55	  "layer2": {
56	    "bead_id": "ddx-12345678",
57	    "base_revision": "abc123",
58	    "result_revision": "def456",
59	    "worktree_path": ".ddx/worktrees/ddx-12345678-run_...",
60	    "finalization": "merge",
61	    "child_run_ids": ["run_20260430T120104Z_01HV..."],
62	    "gate_summary": "go test ./internal/agent"
63	  },
64	  "layer3": null
65	}
66	```
67	
68	Rules:
69	
70	- `layer` is the discriminator; exactly one of `layer1`, `layer2`, or `layer3`
71	  is populated.
72	- `parent_run_id` points upward when a run is nested; child ids live in the
73	  layer extension for efficient drill-down.
74	- `terminal_status` is the substrate outcome. Domain-specific detail remains in
75	  the layer extension and attachments.
76	- `produces_artifact` is set when a run regenerates an artifact; FEAT-007 reads
77	  it as provenance for `generated_by`.
78	- Records are append-only. Correction means writing a new record or a separate
79	  review/annotation artifact, not editing a historical record.
80	
81	## Layer Extensions
82	
83	### Layer 1: invocation atom
84	
85	Layer 1 stores the DDx request envelope and the agent's typed response fields:
86	
87	- prompt attachment or prompt reference
88	- requested `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower`
89	- opaque passthrough constraints (`harness`, `provider`, `model`) when supplied
90	- non-routing work facts such as permissions, effort, timeout, and artifact id
91	- actual model and actual power returned by the agent
92	- token/cost/duration metadata
93	- upstream session id and session-log attachment pointer
94	- structured result attachment
95	
96	DDx does not persist route decisions as DDx-owned policy. Concrete
97	harness/provider/model values are recorded only as requested passthrough or
98	agent-reported facts.
99	
100	### Layer 2: bead attempt
101	
102	Layer 2 stores DDx-owned attempt orchestration:
103	
104	- bead id and governing artifact ids
105	- base revision and worktree path
106	- child layer-1 run ids
107	- commit/result revision, if produced
108	- finalization mode: `merge`, `preserve`, `no_changes`, or `aborted`
109	- preserve ref, if any
110	- gate results and review verdicts
111	- evidence-bundle attachment refs
112	- cleanup summary: removed worktree path, preserve reason, partial setup
113	  cleanup, and resource-preflight result
114	
115	Layer 2 owns success classification for a bead attempt because DDx owns commits,
116	merge/preserve state, no-change rationale, post-run gates, and review evidence.
117	The agent's exit status and actual power are inputs, not the full decision.
118	
119	### Layer 3: queue drain
120	
121	Layer 3 stores the mechanical queue loop:
122	
123	- queue snapshot at start
124	- child layer-2 attempt ids in execution order
125	- retry/escalation decisions with requested `MinPower` changes
126	- no-progress counter state
127	- stop-condition evaluation log
128	- cleanup pass summaries and resource stop details
129	- terminal disposition: `drained`, `blocked`, `deferred`, `no_progress`,
130	  `signal`, or `resource_exhausted`
131	
132	Layer 3 does not file new content-aware work or decide workflow policy.
133	Supervisory behavior belongs to skills or plugins layered on top.
134	
135	## On-Disk Layout
136	
137	```text
138	.ddx/
139	└── runs/
140	    └── <run-id>/
141	        ├── record.json
142	        ├── prompt.md
143	        ├── result.json
144	        ├── session.jsonl
145	        ├── stdout.log
146	        ├── stderr.log
147	        └── evidence/
148	            ├── manifest.json
149	            └── checks.json
150	```
151	
152	Writers create records in a temporary directory and atomically rename into
153	`.ddx/runs/<run-id>/`. A partially written directory is never published as a
154	complete run. Attachment paths in `record.json` are relative to the run
155	directory.
156	
157	## Legacy Migration
158	
159	Two existing layouts are read during the migration window:
160	
161	- `.ddx/exec-runs/` or `.ddx/exec-runs.d/` records become virtual layer-1
162	  records.
163	- `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` bundles become virtual layer-2 records.
164	
165	Migration rules:
166	
167	1. New writes always target `.ddx/runs/`.
168	2. Readers expose legacy records through the same `ddx runs` query model.
169	3. A one-shot rewrite can normalize legacy records into `.ddx/runs/`.
170	4. After the documented cutoff, fallback readers are removed.
171	
172	The migration must preserve git-tracked execute-bead evidence. It must not
173	rewrite historical bead attempt commits or `closing_commit_sha` pointers.
174	
175	## Stop Conditions
176	
177	`ddx work` evaluates stop conditions only between `ddx try` attempts:
178	
179	| Disposition | Trigger |
180	| --- | --- |
181	| `drained` | no ready beads remain |
182	| `blocked` | remaining ready beads are terminal for the current policy |
183	| `deferred` | wall-clock or attempt-count budget exhausted |
184	| `no_progress` | configured consecutive attempts produced no commit and no merged side effect |
185	| `signal` | SIGINT/SIGTERM received; first signal cancels in-flight work cooperatively and stops new claims |
186	| `resource_exhausted` | execution roots are not safely writable or lack required bytes/inodes after cleanup |
187	
188	The layer-3 evaluation log records the candidate bead, previous outcome,
189	retry eligibility, requested power bounds, passthrough envelope presence, and
190	the condition that fired. The log may inspect DDx-owned attempt outcomes and
191	agent typed status; it must not branch on concrete provider/model identity.
192	
193	Signal handling is process-root behavior, not a per-command special case.
194	The root command installs a SIGINT/SIGTERM-backed context. On the first signal,
195	DDx writes `Cancel received, shutting down gracefully` to stderr/stdout visible
196	to the operator, cancels the root context, and lets `ddx work` / `ddx try`
197	finish the TD-031 interruption path: preserve available evidence, remove
198	runtime liveness state, release any non-terminal bead claim, and exit with
199	layer-3 disposition `signal`. A second signal may hard-abort rather than wait
200	for cleanup.
201	
202	## Cleanup Manager
203	
204	The run substrate includes one DDx-owned cleanup manager used by `ddx try`,
205	`ddx work`, server-managed workers, and an explicit operator cleanup command.
206	The manager is scoped to DDx-created execution resources only; it never scans or
207	removes arbitrary temp directories.
208	
209	### Roots and ownership
210	
211	The manager knows the configured temporary worktree root, durable run/evidence
212	roots, worker liveness root, and project git repository. Every created
213	temporary execution directory records enough ownership to answer:
214	
215	- which project created it
216	- which attempt or worker owns it
217	- whether the attempt reached published evidence
218	- whether it was intentionally preserved
219	- when its liveness signal was last refreshed
220	
221	During the legacy migration window, the manager handles both
222	`.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` and `.ddx/runs/<run-id>` evidence, but it treats
223	complete evidence bundles as durable data rather than scratch.
224	DDx-owned cleanup scope also includes DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots,
225	generated test binaries, and run-state or liveness files. Recognized DDx-owned
226	scratch prefixes are: `ddx-exec-wt`, `ddx-claim-heartbeats`,
227	`ddx-metric-keepalive`, `ddx-test-`, and `ddx-e2e-`. Any directory containing
228	a `cleanup.json` metadata file with matching project ownership is DDx-owned
229	regardless of its name. A path is eligible for deletion when: (a) it has
230	`cleanup.json` metadata and the liveness is expired or the owning attempt is
231	terminal; or (b) it matches a recognized DDx prefix without metadata, the
232	directory mtime is at least **6 hours** old, and no live PID or active session
233	is present. The manager preserves published evidence and registered active
234	worktrees.
235	
236	### Entry points
237	
238	Inline cleanup runs inside `ddx try` before and after worktree lifecycle
239	operations. It removes partial directories from failed setup and removes the
240	isolated worktree after merge, preserve, no-changes, no-evidence, failed
241	publish, or cooperative interruption finalization.
242	
243	Loop cleanup runs inside `ddx work` at startup, between attempts after
244	setup/finalization failure, periodically while polling, and during graceful
245	shutdown. It may clean stale resources from prior attempts before the next
246	claim.
247	
248	Background cleanup runs occasionally while long-lived DDx processes are alive.
249	It uses jitter plus a project cleanup lock so concurrent workers do not all
250	prune at once. It must tolerate another process winning the race to remove a
251	resource.
252	
253	An explicit operator command, such as `ddx cleanup` or `ddx doctor cleanup`,
254	runs the same manager and reports what it removed without requiring a queue
255	drain.
256	
257	### Conservative deletion rules
258	
259	The manager may delete:
260	
261	- unregistered directories under DDx temp worktree roots whose names and
262	  metadata identify DDx ownership and whose liveness is absent or stale
263	- registered git worktrees under DDx temp roots whose owning attempt is
264	  terminal or whose liveness marker is stale
265	- DDx-created test and e2e scratch roots that satisfy the same ownership,
266	  age, and liveness rules
267	- generated test binaries and run-state or liveness files that are DDx-owned
268	- stale heartbeat/liveness files for dead PIDs or expired sessions
269	- partial setup directories that never reached atomic evidence publication
270	- old non-preserved scratch data past configured retention
271	
272	The manager must not delete:
273	
274	- preserved attempt worktrees
275	- local evidence refs under `refs/ddx/iterations/...`
276	- complete `.ddx/runs/<id>` or `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>` evidence
277	- active worktrees with a live PID/session heartbeat
278	- paths outside configured DDx roots
279	- paths that only loosely resemble DDx names without matching ownership
280	  metadata or registered worktree evidence
281	
282	### Resource preflight and loop-fatal handling
283	
284	Before `ddx try` claims a bead or creates a worktree, it checks the configured
285	temp worktree root and durable evidence root for writability, free bytes, and
286	free inodes when inode data is available. If preflight fails, `ddx try` runs one
287	immediate cleanup pass and repeats the check. If the check still fails, the
288	attempt returns `resource_exhausted` without claiming the bead.
289	
290	`ddx work` and server-managed workers perform the same cleanup pass before the
291	first claim and before any later claim when any checked temp or evidence root
292	falls below the soft cleanup trigger of **512 MiB free bytes** or **8192 free
293	inodes**. If the temp roots remain below the hard stop floor of **64 MiB free
294	bytes** and **1024 free inodes** after that cleanup, the loop records host
295	exhaustion and stops claiming new beads.
296	
297	If resource exhaustion occurs after a claim or during worktree setup,
298	`ddx try` records whatever partial evidence is available, removes any partial
299	unregistered directory it created, releases the claim if the bead did not close,
300	and returns `resource_exhausted`.
301	
302	`ddx work` treats `resource_exhausted` as host infrastructure failure. It
303	records a layer-3 stop-condition entry, prints an operator-visible message such
304	as `resource exhausted after cleanup; stopping work loop`, and stops claiming
305	new beads. It must not continue scanning the ready queue in the same process.
306	
307	Cleanup output is structured. Routine passes are trace/debug or worker events.
308	Passes that reclaim meaningful bytes or inodes emit an operator-visible summary
309	including counts. Failures include path, class, and whether the failure blocked
310	progress.
311	
312	Expected implementation tests include `TestExecutionCleanup_RemovesStaleDDXScratchDirs`,
313	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_RunsCleanupBelowSoftFloor`, and
314	`TestWorkResourcePreflight_StopsBelowHardFloorAfterCleanup`.
315	
316	## Drill-Down Query Model
317	
318	The read API is centered on `ddx runs`:
319	
320	- `ddx runs list --layer 1|2|3 --bead <id> --artifact <id>`
321	- `ddx runs show <run-id>`
322	- `ddx runs children <run-id>`
323	- `ddx runs parents <run-id>`
324	- `ddx runs log <run-id>`
325	- `ddx runs result <run-id>`
326	
327	Server and MCP surfaces mirror those reads. A layer-3 detail page drills down
328	to child layer-2 attempts; each layer-2 attempt drills down to child layer-1
329	invocations. The graph is navigated by `parent_run_id` and child id arrays, not
330	by path guessing.
331	
332	## Implementation Notes
333	
334	- Reuse the existing execution attachment discipline where it still fits, but
335	  do not keep `.ddx/exec-runs` and `.ddx/executions` as peer authoritative
336	  writers.
337	- Keep passthrough constraints in one request/record subobject so harness,
338	  provider, and model do not spread through queue, retry, or scheduling code.
339	- Treat artifact regeneration as metadata on layer 1 or layer 2, depending on
340	  whether the generator returns content directly or edits the repo in a
341	  worktree.
342	- Add tests at the storage boundary for atomic publish, legacy read
343	  compatibility, parent/child traversal, and no-progress stop-condition
344	  recording.

10	# Solution Design: Multi-Machine Land Coordinator Topology
11	
12	## Purpose
13	
14	Define the operator-visible contract for running `ddx-server` on multiple
15	machines against the same project. Each machine runs its own land coordinator;
16	a shared git remote is the serialization point that prevents conflicting
17	landings without any machine-to-machine coordination protocol.
18	
19	## Scope
20	
21	- One land coordinator per machine × project
22	- Push-to-origin as the distributed serialization point
23	- Conflict, divergence, and recovery paths
24	- Operator setup and daily commands
25	
26	Out of scope:
27	
28	- Cross-machine `beads.jsonl` replication (see ddx-e2f497c3 for framing)
29	- Cross-machine worker supervision or bead-claim visibility
30	- Cross-machine observability aggregation (see ddx-e2f497c3)
31	- Cross-machine beads.jsonl concurrency (see ddx-2791dc4f)
32	
33	## Topology Model
34	
35	Each machine that participates in execute-loop work runs one `ddx-server`
36	process. That server hosts one land coordinator goroutine per registered
37	project. The coordinator owns the fetch → (fast-forward or merge) → push
38	sequence for all landings on that machine. When the target has not advanced
39	since the worker started, Land() fast-forwards via `update-ref`. When the
40	target has advanced (because a sibling worker on either machine landed
41	first), Land() creates a `--no-ff` merge commit so the worker's original
42	commit is never rewritten and replay fidelity is preserved.
43	
44	```
45	  eitri                           bragi
46	  ┌──────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────┐
47	  │  ddx-server      │            │  ddx-server      │
48	  │  land coordinator│            │  land coordinator│
49	  │  (project: ddx)  │            │  (project: ddx)  │
50	  └────────┬─────────┘            └────────┬─────────┘
51	           │ git push --ff-only             │ git push --ff-only
52	           └──────────────┐  ┌─────────────┘
53	                          ▼  ▼
54	                     origin (shared remote)
55	                     single source of truth
56	                     for target branch
57	```
58	
59	Both coordinators are autonomous. Neither knows about the other. The remote
60	serializes them through the atomicity of `git push`.
61	
62	## Setup
63	
64	Each machine needs:
65	
66	1. A local clone of the project with `origin` pointing at the shared remote.
67	2. `ddx server` running (or enabled as a user service per FEAT-002 and SD-019).
68	3. The machine's `ddx-server` registered with the project root so the land
69	   coordinator starts.
70	
71	No additional configuration is required. Machines do not need to reach each
72	other; they only need write access to the shared `origin`.
73	
74	## Happy Path
75	
76	1. Worker on machine A claims a bead (bead is marked claimed in local
77	   `beads.jsonl`; not yet visible on machine B).
78	2. Worker executes the bead in an isolated worktree.
79	3. Land coordinator on machine A: `git fetch origin`, compare local target
80	   tip to the worker's BaseRev. When equal, fast-forward the local branch

10	# Feature: Multi-Machine Sync
11	
12	**ID:** FEAT-023
13	**Status:** Implemented
14	**Priority:** P1
15	**Owner:** DDx Team
16	
17	## Overview
18	
19	`ddx sync` is a first-class CLI command that synchronizes DDx-managed files
20	(`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/plugins/`) with `origin/main`
21	in a single canonical flow. It exists in both one-shot and watch (daemon)
22	modes and is constrained to never touch any path outside the DDx-managed
23	allowlist.
24	
25	Sync solves the multi-machine drift problem operators see when DDx is checked
26	out on more than one machine: workers churn `beads.jsonl` every few minutes
27	during execute-loop drains, the working tree is constantly dirty in tracked
28	paths, and manually pulling/stashing/popping/committing/pushing is friction
29	that gets skipped, causing tracker divergence.
30	
31	## Problem Statement
32	
33	**Current situation:** Operators with DDx on multiple machines manually run
34	the sequence `git fetch && git stash && git pull && git stash pop && git add
35	.ddx/beads.jsonl && git commit -m "tracker" && git push` (with execution
36	evidence variants) several times per session to keep tracker state aligned.
37	A 2026-04-29 dogfood session ran this flow ~6 times in ~3 hours.
38	
39	**Pain points:**
40	
41	- Manual sync is constant friction; missing a sync risks divergent tracker
42	  state between machines.
43	- No remote-only solution exists: cloud routines cannot touch a local
44	  working tree, and a server-side sync only helps if every endpoint is the
45	  server (DDx is per-project local).
46	- Operators forget to sync, then have to reconcile divergent
47	  `beads.jsonl` lines by hand.
48	
49	**Desired outcome:** A single `ddx sync` keystroke (or zero, with
50	`--watch`) performs the canonical flow safely. Anything that cannot be
51	auto-resolved — stash-pop conflict, double push failure, divergent base —
52	aborts cleanly with a structured non-zero exit, writes a failure record,
53	and surfaces through `ddx doctor` so the operator notices without having
54	to remember to look.
55	
56	## Scope
57	
58	### In Scope
59	
60	- `ddx sync` one-shot command running the canonical flow:
61	  1. `git fetch origin`
62	  2. Stash tracked dirty files in the DDx-managed allowlist (untracked
63	     additions are not stashed; merge does not touch them).
64	  3. `git merge origin/main` (no rebase — preserves execute-bead history
65	     per the project's standing merge policy).
66	  4. `git stash pop` if a stash was created.
67	  5. Commit DDx-managed dirty paths with structured messages:
68	     `.ddx/beads.jsonl` → `chore: tracker`;
69	     `.ddx/executions/` and `.ddx/plugins/` → `chore: add execution evidence`.
70	  6. `git push origin main`; on non-fast-forward, retry from step 1 once.
71	- `ddx sync --watch [--interval=15m]` running the same flow on an
72	  interval until interrupted. Local-only, foreground process; the operator
73	  is responsible for backgrounding.
74	- Strict allowlist enforcement: only `.ddx/beads.jsonl`,
75	  `.ddx/executions/`, and `.ddx/plugins/` are ever stashed, staged, or
76	  committed. Unrelated dirty changes survive untouched across the entire
77	  flow.
78	- No destructive flags: sync never invokes git with `--force`,
79	  `--no-verify`, `--hard`, or any other destructive option.
80	- Structured abort: when any step cannot be auto-resolved (stash-pop
81	  conflict, double push fail, fetch/merge failure), sync exits non-zero,
82	  writes `.ddx/sync-failure.json` containing timestamp and reason, and
83	  leaves the working tree in a state the operator can inspect.
84	- Doctor integration: `ddx doctor` reads `.ddx/sync-failure.json` (when
85	  present) and reports a `sync_aborted` diagnostic with timestamp, reason,
86	  and remediation steps.
87	- Cross-platform: works on macOS and Linux. No symlinks, no shell-isms;
88	  implemented in Go using the existing `cli/internal/git` shell-out
89	  helpers.
90	
91	### Out of Scope
92	
93	- Conflict resolution beyond stash-pop (the preserved-iteration problem
94	  is tracked separately in ddx-0097af14).
95	- Sync of paths outside the DDx-managed allowlist — by design, sync
96	  never touches user code.
97	- Multi-remote sync. v1 supports a single `origin` only.
98	- Server-loop integration: when ddx-server runs continuously per-project,
99	  it can call `ddx sync` as part of its loop. The hook is not part of v1
100	  but the command is designed to be re-entrant and safe for that use.
101	
102	## Acceptance Criteria
103	
104	1. `ddx sync` exists and runs the canonical flow described above.
105	2. `ddx sync --watch [--interval=15m]` runs the flow on the configured
106	   interval until killed.
107	3. Both modes refuse to touch paths outside the DDx-managed allowlist
108	   (`.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/executions/`, `.ddx/plugins/`). Verified by
109	   tests that inject unrelated dirty changes and assert they survive
110	   untouched.
111	4. Both modes never invoke git with `--force`, `--no-verify`, or any
112	   destructive flag. Verified by tests asserting on the captured shell
113	   args.
114	5. Stash-pop conflict and double push failure abort with a structured
115	   non-zero exit and write `.ddx/sync-failure.json`. Covered by tests
116	   using a fake git runner.
117	6. `ddx doctor` surfaces a recent sync failure as a `sync_aborted`
118	   diagnostic when `.ddx/sync-failure.json` is present.
119	7. Tests run green on macOS and Linux.
120	
121	## Implementation Notes
122	
123	The command lives in `cli/cmd/sync.go` with tests in `cli/cmd/sync_test.go`.
124	Git invocations route through a `syncGitRunner` function type so tests can
125	inject a fake without spawning real git processes. The allowlist is encoded
126	as a package-level `ddxManagedPaths` slice; every status check, stash, add,
127	and commit references that slice rather than building paths ad hoc.
128	
129	`ddx doctor` integration is via `checkSyncFailure(failurePath)` which reads
130	the JSON record (if any) and returns a `DiagnosticIssue` with remediation
131	guidance.

173	## Requirements
174	
175	### Functional
176	
177	**Early commit (S1, S3, S4, S7)**
178	1. DDx operations that write tracked files can auto-commit the change
179	2. Auto-commit is configurable: `always` (default for MCP/UI writes),

199	14. `ddx bead` mutating commands auto-commit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` after
200	    successful operations (governed by `git.auto_commit`)

217	**Bead-Attempt Git Operations**
218	
219	The general DDx git safety posture is conservative (see SD-012). One managed
220	exception exists: `ddx try` requires a controlled set of git operations beyond
221	the read-plus-commit baseline. These are explicitly permitted only within the
222	bead-attempt workflow:
223	
224	22. Checkpoint a dirty caller worktree before execution (creates a commit, does
225	    not discard changes)
226	23. Create an isolated temporary worktree from the resolved base revision
227	24. Rebase the execution branch onto the latest target branch tip — only to
228	    prepare a fast-forward landing, never for history rewriting
229	25. Fast-forward update the target branch when merge conditions are satisfied
230	26. Preserve non-landed iterations under hidden refs using the naming scheme:
231	    `refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<timestamp>-<base-shortsha>` where
232	    `<timestamp>` is `YYYYMMDDTHHMMSSZ` (UTC compact ISO-8601, e.g.,
233	    `20260408T130000Z`) and `<base-shortsha>` is at least 12 characters
234	27. **No-orphan-worktree invariant:** Execution worktrees are always removed
235	    after the workflow completes (success, failure, or crash recovery); a crash
236	    during cleanup must not leave a persistent orphan worktree; the next
237	    execute-bead invocation detects and removes orphaned worktrees matching the
238	    execute-bead path pattern before proceeding
239	28. Hidden refs are local and not pushed by DDx; preserved iterations can be
240	    enumerated with `git for-each-ref 'refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/*'`
241	29. Epic-scoped execution may create one persistent managed worktree and one
242	    branch named after the epic, such as `ddx/epics/<epic-id>`, and reuse that
243	    worktree across sequential child-bead executions for that epic
244	30. Child-bead commits on an epic branch are ordinary commits on that branch;
245	    they are not fast-forward landed directly to the target branch
246	31. The completed epic branch lands to the target branch with a regular merge
247	    commit so the child-bead commit history remains intact
248	32. Epic merge-gate executions run against the merge candidate before the merge
249	    commit is finalized
250	33. Epic worktrees are long-lived only for the lifetime of an active epic
251	    worker; once the epic is merged, abandoned, or reset, DDx must remove the
252	    managed epic worktree and leave no orphaned epic worktree behind
253	34. **Tracked execution-evidence bundle.** Each execute-bead attempt writes
254	    a tracked bundle at `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` containing at least
255	    `prompt.md`, `manifest.json`, and `result.json` per FEAT-006
256	    §"Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle". The bundle is committed as part of the
257	    iteration (landed or preserved under the hidden ref in requirement 26).
258	    The DDx default `.gitignore` template and git safety posture must not
259	    exclude `.ddx/executions/` from tracking; only the ignored runtime

255	    `prompt.md`, `manifest.json`, and `result.json` per FEAT-006
256	    §"Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle". The bundle is committed as part of the
257	    iteration (landed or preserved under the hidden ref in requirement 26).
258	    The DDx default `.gitignore` template and git safety posture must not
259	    exclude `.ddx/executions/` from tracking; only the ignored runtime
260	    scratch paths listed in FEAT-006 may be excluded.
261	35. **Canonical commit provenance trailers.** Each iteration commit
262	    (landed or preserved) carries the canonical Git trailer set defined in
263	    FEAT-006 §"Canonical Git trailers": `Ddx-Attempt-Id`, `Ddx-Worker-Id`,
264	    `Ddx-Harness`, `Ddx-Model`, and `Ddx-Result-Status`. The git layer must
265	    preserve these trailers verbatim on rebase+fast-forward landing and on
266	    hidden-ref preservation; it must not rewrite, strip, or reorder them.
267	    Consumers of commit history rely on these trailer names as the stable
268	    provenance surface.
269	
270	All other DDx git operations remain conservative: DDx does not force-push,
271	rebase outside execute-bead, delete branches, or amend commits outside this
272	managed flow.
273	
274	**Epic branch and worktree naming rules**
275	
276	Requirements 29–33 above establish that DDx may create a persistent epic
277	worktree and branch. The following naming and persistence rules pin down the
278	contract so external tools can observe epic state deterministically:
279	
280	34. The epic branch name is `ddx/epics/<epic-id>`. The segment `<epic-id>` is
281	    the tracker id of the epic bead. DDx must not reuse this branch name for
282	    anything other than that epic.
283	35. The epic branch is created from the resolved base revision on the target
284	    branch the first time the epic worker launches for that epic, and is
285	    reused across subsequent launches of the same worker until the epic is
286	    merged, abandoned, or reset.
287	36. The epic worktree path follows a single managed pattern derived from the
288	    epic id so crash-recovery logic can match orphans by path. The worktree
289	    is attached to the epic branch and lives until the epic worker exits
290	    cleanly or the epic is reset.
291	37. Child-bead commits on the epic branch are ordinary commits (requirement
292	    30). A child bead may be closed in the tracker as soon as its acceptance
293	    and required gates pass in the epic branch context, even though its
294	    commit still lives only on `ddx/epics/<epic-id>` and has not yet reached
295	    the target branch. "Closed on epic" means the child work is finalized on
296	    the epic branch and is waiting for the epic merge, not that it has
297	    landed on the target branch.
298	38. Final integration of a completed epic uses `git merge --no-ff` from
299	    `ddx/epics/<epic-id>` into the target branch so the full child-commit
300	    history remains visible in target history. Fast-forwarding an epic
301	    branch into the target branch is not permitted.
302	39. Epic merge gates run against the merge candidate — the epic branch tip
303	    after it has been rebased onto the latest target tip — not against
304	    individual child commits. The merge commit is only created after those
305	    gates pass.
306	40. A failed epic merge gate preserves the epic branch and worktree for
307	    operator inspection; DDx must not silently discard an epic branch that
308	    failed to merge.
309	
310	### Non-Functional
311	
312	- **Safety:** Never force-push, rebase, or delete branches — except within the
313	  execute-bead managed flow (see requirements 22–28). Outside execute-bead, DDx
314	  only creates commits, tags, and reads history. Epic execution adds one
315	  further managed exception: a regular merge commit is permitted only when DDx
316	  lands a completed epic branch under the epic worker contract.
317	- **Performance:** History queries should use `--follow` for renamed files.
318	  Commit operations <500ms.
319	- **Compatibility:** Works with any git repo. No special git configuration
320	  required.
321	- **Graceful degradation:** If not in a git repo, history/commit features
322	  are disabled with clear messages. Core DDx functionality still works.
323	
324	## Affected Existing Specs
325	
326	| Spec | Change Needed |
327	|------|--------------|
328	| FEAT-002 (Server) | Remove "read-only for v1" constraint. Add write+commit endpoints for documents. |
329	| FEAT-007 (Doc Graph) | Remove "use git directly" from Out of Scope. Add history/diff/changed commands. |
330	| PRD | Add git awareness as a primary capability. |
331	
332	## User Stories
333	
334	### US-120: Agent Edits Are Protected by Auto-Commit
335	**As an** AI agent editing spec documents
336	**I want** DDx to auto-commit each document change
337	**So that** my edits survive session boundaries and crashes
338	
339	**Acceptance Criteria:**
340	- Given auto-commit is enabled, when I write a document via MCP, then a git
341	  commit is created with the artifact ID in the message
342	- Given two agents edit different documents, then each edit is a separate
343	  commit with clear attribution
344	
345	### US-121: Developer Views Document History
346	**As a** developer reviewing a spec
347	**I want** to see how it evolved over recent commits
348	**So that** I understand what changed and why
349	
350	**Acceptance Criteria:**
351	- Given I run `ddx doc history FEAT-001`, then I see a log of commits that
352	  touched the FEAT-001 file with dates and messages
353	- Given I run `ddx doc diff FEAT-001 v0.1.0`, then I see the content diff
354	  since that tag
355	
356	### US-122: MCP Client Writes and Commits a Document
357	**As an** MCP-connected agent
358	**I want** to update a document and have it committed atomically
359	**So that** my changes are durable and visible to other tools
360	
361	**Acceptance Criteria:**
362	- Given I call `ddx_doc_write` with an artifact ID and content, then the file
363	  is written and committed
364	- Given the commit fails (e.g., merge conflict), then I receive a clear error
365	  and the working tree is not left in a dirty state
366	
367	### US-123: Workflow Creates Checkpoints
368	**As a** workflow tool
369	**I want** to tag a known-good state before risky operations
370	**So that** I can restore if something goes wrong
371	
372	**Acceptance Criteria:**
373	- Given I run `ddx checkpoint pre-build`, then a git tag `ddx/pre-build`
374	  is created
375	- Given I run `ddx checkpoint --restore pre-build`, then the working tree
376	  matches the tagged state
377	
378	### US-124: Bead Changes Are Auto-Committed
379	**As an** AI agent managing work items
380	**I want** bead mutations to be committed automatically
381	**So that** tracker state survives session boundaries and is visible to other
382	agents and developers
383	
384	**Acceptance Criteria:**
385	- Given auto-commit is enabled, when I run `ddx bead create "Fix login"`,
386	  then `.ddx/beads.jsonl` is committed with a structured message
387	- Given I run `ddx bead close hx-001`, then the bead state change is committed
388	- Given I run `ddx bead import`, then one commit is created for the entire
389	  batch, not one per record
390	- Given auto-commit is `never`, then bead commands do not create commits
391	
392	### US-125: Init Generates Agent Guidance for Committing DDx State
393	**As a** developer setting up a project
394	**I want** `ddx init` to instruct agents about which files to commit
395	**So that** agents don't leave DDx state files uncommitted
396	
397	**Acceptance Criteria:**
398	- Given I run `ddx init`, then CLAUDE.md contains guidance listing
399	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl`, `.ddx/config.yaml`, `.agents/skills/`, and
400	  `.claude/skills/` as files that should be committed
401	- Given I run `ddx init --force`, then the guidance is refreshed
402	- Given an agent reads the generated guidance, then it knows to `git add`
403	  and commit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` after bead operations
404	
405	### US-126: Bead-Attempt Git Lifecycle Is Safe and Contained
406	**As** a developer running `ddx try`
407	**I want** the git operations to be controlled and leave my repo in a predictable state
408	**So that** my working tree is never lost or corrupted by a bead-attempt run
409	
410	**Acceptance Criteria:**
411	- Given the caller's working tree has uncommitted changes when `ddx try` starts, when the workflow begins, then DDx creates a checkpoint commit from those changes and uses it as the effective base revision — the caller's staged and unstaged changes are not discarded or reset.
412	- Given `ddx try` runs, when it begins, then DDx creates a managed isolated worktree; when execution completes (success, failure, or crash recovery), then no worktree created by that attempt remains in the filesystem.
413	- Given an iteration is merge-eligible, when DDx prepares a fast-forward landing, then the only rebase performed is a rebase of the execution branch onto the latest target branch tip — `git log --merges` shows no merge commit; history remains linear.
414	- Given an iteration is not merged (required execution failed, ratchet regression, or `--no-merge` set), when DDx preserves the iteration, then a ref matching `refs/ddx/iterations/<bead-id>/<timestamp>-<base-shortsha>` is created and the target branch is not updated.
415	- Given `ddx try` left an orphan worktree due to a crash, when the next `ddx try` invocation starts, then DDx detects and removes orphaned worktrees matching the attempt path pattern before proceeding.
416	- Given two `ddx try` invocations on the same bead run concurrently or in rapid succession from the same base, then each produces a distinct hidden ref because the `YYYYMMDDTHHMMSSZ-<12charsha>` combination is unique per invocation; DDx does not serialize or lock across concurrent invocations.
417	
418	## Dependencies
419	
420	- `internal/git` package (existing — basic git operations)
421	- FEAT-004 (beads — bead mutation operations)
422	- FEAT-007 (document graph — artifact ID to file path mapping)
423	- FEAT-009 (plugin registry — `ddx init` and `ddx install`)
424	
425	Note: FEAT-002 (Server) depends on FEAT-012, not the reverse. FEAT-012 defines
426	the git mechanics (write+commit, history, checkpoints); FEAT-002 exposes those
427	capabilities as HTTP and MCP endpoints. The dependency runs FEAT-002 → FEAT-012.
428	
429	## Out of Scope
430	
431	- Branch management (creating, switching, merging branches)
432	- Remote operations (push, pull, fetch)
433	- Merge conflict resolution (report conflicts, don't resolve them)
434	- Git configuration management
435	- Submodule or subtree operations (handled by existing `ddx update/contribute`)
436	- **When to invoke execute-bead and what to do with the outcome** — DDx provides the git mechanics; workflow tools decide when to run execute-bead, whether to retry, and how to act on preserved vs landed iterations
437	- **Conflict classification and escalation** — whether a merge conflict is resolvable or requires escalation is a workflow tool policy decision, not a DDx git operation

Define the operator-visible contract for running `ddx-server` on multiple
machines against the same project. Each machine runs its own land coordinator;
a shared git remote is the serialization point that prevents conflicting
landings without any machine-to-machine coordination protocol.

> **Single-node vs multi-machine:** By default, `ddx try` / `ddx work` operate
> in single-node mode (FEAT-010 default): `Land()` performs only a local

> and applies only when multiple machines share a git remote as a serialization
> point. Single-node drain never calls `git fetch` or `git push` as part of

## P9: Network-Free Drain

The single-node drain loop must never block on network I/O. `Land()` performs
only a local worktree-merge into the local target branch under a brief local
lock. `git fetch` and `git push` are never called from `Land()` or from the
`ddx work` queue loop.

This invariant prevents the 30-120 s lock holds that caused ddx-08e90869 (work
landed only on a preserved iteration ref due to tracker lock contention) and
ddx-15a51f8c (push-race remediation). Network operations are operator-driven
(see FEAT-023 `ddx sync`) or future background-deferred, never coupled to the
drain.

Governing requirement: FEAT-010 §"Network-free drain boundary" (functional
requirement 16) and §"Network isolation" (non-functional). Implementation
target: `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_land.go` `Land()` and
`cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go` drain loop — neither must call
`git fetch` or `git push`.
